request.question: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?

title: Norm Clarke
Jonathan Landman
A. Charles Muller
Hail Flutie
Martin Gottlieb
China Economic Information Network
Richard Stolley
Bill Adee
Doug Flutie
Brent Musburger
sentences: Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.
He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.
He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.
His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."
Jonathan I. Landman is an American journalist and deputy managing editor at "The New York Times".
Landman became deputy managing editor responsible for digital journalism for "The Times" in August 2005.
He had become assistant managing editor and member of "The Times" masthead in May 2005.
Prior to that, Landman had been acting culture editor from 2004 to 2005.
Previously, he was the assistant managing editor for enterprise from 2003 to 2004.
Landman had been metropolitan editor of "The Times" since 1999 and editor of "The Times"<nowiki>'</nowiki> Week in Review section since 1994.
Before that, he served as deputy editor of the Washington bureau since 1992; assistant editor in Washington from 1991; assistant metropolitan editor from 1990 and assistant national editor from 1989.
Landman joined "The Times" as a copy editor in 1987.
A. Charles Muller (born September 19, 1953) is an academic specializing in Korean Buddhism and East Asian Yogacara, having published numerous books and articles on these topics.
He is a resident of Japan, currently teaching at the University of Tokyo.

He is one of the earliest and most prolific developers of online research resources for the field of Buddhist Studies, being the founder and managing editor of the online Digital Dictionary of Buddhism, the CJKV-English Dictionary, and the H-Buddhism Scholars Information Network, along with having digitized and published numerous reference works.
The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.
It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.
The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.
Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.
Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.
The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.
Martin Gottlieb is Assistant Managing Editor/Investigations of "Newsday".
From 2012 to 2016, he was Editor of "The Record" of Bergen County, New Jersey.
Previously, from 2008 to 2011, he was Global Editions Editor of the "New York Times"; in this capacity, he oversaw production of the "International Herald Tribune".
Gottlieb also served as Associate Managing Editor, Deputy Culture Editor and National Project Editor of the "Times".
Gottlieb was also Managing Editor of the "New York Daily News" and Editor-in-Chief of "The Village Voice".
China Economic Information Network (中国经济信息网) is a national information network that provides information and analyses of Chinese national macroeconomic trends.
It is sponsored by the State Information Network.
Richard Brockway Stolley (born October 3, 1928) is an American journalist and magazine editor.
He's most well known for his work at "Time" magazine, which he joined in 1953.
He subsequently held a number of roles at the magazine including report, writer, bureau chief, senior editor and managing editor.

In addition to his work at "Time", he has also been Assistant Managing Editor and Managing Editor of "Life" magazine, the founding Managing Editor of "People" magazine and Director of Special Projects for Time Inc
Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.
Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".
He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.

He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).
He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.
His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.
Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.
Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.
In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.
Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).
He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.

Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.
He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.
He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?
hop1_docs: Vegas Stats & Information Network | VSiN is a national sports gambling news network based in Las Vegas, Nevada.  VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career.  Musburger left ESPN in January 2017 and joined VSiN.

Brent Musburger | Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).  He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.  Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.  He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.  He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Hail Flutie | The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.  It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.  The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.  Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.  Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.  The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.

Stat (system call) | stat() is a Unix system call that returns file attributes about an inode.  The semantics of stat() vary between operating systems.  As an example, Unix command ls uses this system call to retrieve information on files that includes:

Norm Clarke | Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.  He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.  He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.  His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."

Bill Adee | Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.  Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".  He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.  He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.

- Brent Musburger — [2] "lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN)."
- Brent Musburger — [4] "with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating."
- Hail Flutie game — [4] "The Hail Flutie game" ; date: [4] "November 23, 1984."

Ambiguities:
- None identified. "Brent Musburger" uniquely matches the managing editor role and is listed as a commentator for the Hail Flutie game.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts — passages show the same person (Brent Musburger) is both a VSiN managing editor and a commentator on the Hail Flutie game. Final conclusion may be given.

Retrieval direction:
- "Brent Musburger" (Wikipedia page)
- "Hail Flutie" (Wikipedia page)
- "Vegas Stats & Information Network" (VSiN Wikipedia page)
Conclusion: Brent Musburger.

response.summary_2: Brent Musburger, the lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN), was one of the commentators calling the action for the Hail Flutie game.
